did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used artificial neural networks (ANNs), specifically a convolutional neural network (CNN) and an attention-based multiple instance learning (MIL) classifier, to detect and classify basal cell carcinomas (BCCs) in histological whole-slide images (WSIs). The AI models were trained to identify tumor regions and differentiate BCCs from normal skin, with performance metrics reported (AUC: 0.993). The study also compared the recognition patterns of the ANN with those of pathologists using eye-tracking data.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is labeled as an 'ARTICLE' and describes a 'proof-of-concept study' where the authors implemented and trained artificial neural networks (ANNs) on a dataset of 820 histological whole-slide images. It includes original methods (data collection, neural network architecture, eye-tracking comparison), results (AUC, sensitivity, specificity metrics), and a discussion of new findings regarding recognition patterns, which classifies it as original experimental research rather than a review.

title: Artificial neural networks and pathologists recognize basal cell carcinomas based on different histological patterns
brief_summary: This proof-of-concept study evaluated the ability of artificial neural networks (ANNs) to detect and classify basal cell carcinomas (BCCs) in histological whole-slide images (WSIs). The researchers trained an attention-based multiple instance learning classifier on a dataset of 820 H&E-stained WSIs (601 BCCs and 219 normal skin samples) retrospectively collected from two Austrian hospitals. The ANN achieved high diagnostic accuracy, with an area under the ROC curve of 0.993, sensitivity of 0.965, and specificity of 0.910. To assess interpretability, the study compared the histological regions identified by the ANN's attention mechanism with the gaze patterns of pathologists using eye-tracking techniques. The results revealed that machine learning algorithms rely on significantly different recognition patterns for tumor identification compared to expert pathologists (p < 10^-4). The authors concluded that while ANNs can efficiently analyze histopathological images, they utilize distinct classification patterns that differ from human diagnostic strategies.

lesion_types_studied.details: Primary target of the study; 601 WSIs containing BCCs were used in the dataset. The ANN was trained to detect and classify these as positive cases.

Negative control class; 219 WSIs showing only normal skin were used. These samples were derived from surgical excisions in proximity to various skin neoplasms (BCC, SCC, melanoma) or were scars/dog ears.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnosis of Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) vs. non-BCC (normal skin) on whole-slide images (WSIs).
all_task_types.details: The study implemented an attention-based multiple instance learning (MIL) classifier and a baseline CNN to classify WSIs as containing BCC or normal skin. The task is binary classification (BCC vs. non-BCC). Performance was evaluated using AUC (0.993), sensitivity (0.965), and specificity (0.910). The model implicitly calculated a weight matrix to identify tumor regions, but the primary output was a class label for the slide.

datasets_used.name: Retrospective Austrian Hospital Cohort
datasets_used.description: A collection of 820 H&E-stained histological whole-slide images (WSIs) retrospectively collected from two Austrian hospitals: Kepler University Hospital Linz and the Medical University of Vienna. The dataset includes metadata such as diagnosis, patient age, gender, and pseudonymous patient ID. Samples were categorized by two board-certified pathologists.

use.training: 688 images (84% of total) were used for training the artificial neural networks (CNN and attention-based MIL classifier).
use.validation: 20% of the training set (138 images) was used for validation during hyperparameter tuning.
use.testing: 132 images (16% of total) were used as the test set for evaluating model performance (AUC, sensitivity, specificity).
use.other: A subset of 9 images (5 BCC and 4 non-BCC) randomly selected from the test set was used for the eye-tracking study with pathologists.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To assess the robustness of the ANN methods and calculate means and standard deviations for performance metrics (accuracy, F1-score, AUC).
To calculate the statistical significance of the performance metrics (AUC, F1-score, accuracy) comparing the proposed attention-ANN method against other ANN methods (MIL-max, MIL-mean, baseline CNN).
To calculate the statistical significance of the similarity metrics (Jaccard and Dice scores) comparing the recognition patterns of the ANN with those of pathologists.
To calculate correlations between two variables (specifically, the relationship between spatial proximity to neoplasms and classification bias of non-BCC samples).
To report uncertainty for sensitivity, specificity, and AUC metrics of the attention-ANN.
To measure the similarity between the regions of interest identified by the ANN's attention mechanism and the gaze patterns of pathologists.
To measure the similarity between the regions of interest identified by the ANN's attention mechanism and the gaze patterns of pathologists.

hypothesis_testing.test: Two-tailed Wilcoxon signed-rank test (for ANN performance metrics); Two-tailed independent sample t-test (for similarity metrics between ANN and pathologists)
hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no difference in the performance metrics (AUC, F1-score, accuracy) between the attention-ANN and other ANN methods; There is no difference in the similarity of recognition patterns (Jaccard and Dice scores) between the ANN and pathologists compared to the similarity between pathologists themselves.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant difference in the performance metrics between the attention-ANN and other ANN methods; There is a significant difference in the similarity of recognition patterns between the ANN and pathologists compared to the similarity between pathologists themselves.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The study found that machine learning algorithms rely on significantly different recognition patterns for tumor identification compared to pathologists (p < 10^-4). Specifically, the Jaccard similarity between pathologists and the ANN was significantly lower than the similarity between pathologists (p = 5.81 × 10^-15), and the Sørensen-Dice coefficient showed a similar significant difference (p = 1.10 × 10^-16). The attention-ANN method also showed statistically significant improvements in AUC, F1-score, and accuracy compared to other ANN methods (p < 0.05).

Retrospective Austrian Hospital Cohort (.description: Test set consisting of 132 whole-slide images (WSIs): 97 Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) and 35 non-BCC (normal skin). Results are reported as median with interquartile range (IQR) from 100 times retrained models.
Retrospective Austrian Hospital Cohort (.notes: MIL-attention significantly outperformed MIL-max, MIL-mean, and baseline CNN regarding AUC, F1-score, and accuracy (p < 0.05). MIL-attention identified BCC regions with an accuracy of 0.950 ± 0.008 (SD) across 100 retrainings.

Misclassification Analysis (Attention-AN.description: Analysis of WSIs misclassified by at least 1 of 100 retrained attention-ANNs. BCCs were mainly misclassified due to small parts of BCC specimen. Non-BCC misclassifications were associated with solar elastosis, inflammation, scar, fibrosis, or high vascularization.

Eye-Tracking Comparison (ANN vs. Patholo.notes: Machine learning algorithms rely on significantly different recognition patterns for tumor identification compared to expert pathologists (p < 10^-4). Pathologists focused on epidermal structures, while the ANN paid additional attention to connective tissue patterns.

architecture_used.main_model: Two architectures were implemented: a baseline CNN and an attention-based Multiple Instance Learning (MIL) classifier. The primary proposed model is the MIL-attention classifier.
architecture_used.pretraining: The feature constructor CNN within the MIL architecture used a VGG11 network pre-trained on ImageNet.
architecture_used.input_size: Baseline CNN: downsized WSIs of 1024 × 1024 pixels. MIL Architecture: WSIs split into tiles of 224 × 224 pixels.

baseline_cnn.input_processing: Downscaled WSIs (1024 × 1024 pixels) with white padding

mil_attention.type: Multiple Instance Learning (MIL) with attention classifier

feature_constructor.tile_processing: Empty tiles removed via pixel statistics; nonempty tiles normalized to 0 mean and unit variance; mini-batches of 32

classification_head.type: Attention classifier (based on Ilse et al.)

training_details.hyperparameter_tuning: Manual hyperparameter search/tuning on a validation set
training_details.validation_strategy: 20% of the training set used for validation

classification_or_output_head.mil_attention: Attention-based pooling over tile representations to produce slide-level prediction

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Multiple Instance Learning with mean pooling (MIL-mean)
Baseline Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with SELU activation (baseline SELU CNN)
Feature constructor CNN (VGG11 pre-trained on ImageNet)
Classification ANN (based on Ilse et al.)
preprocessing_techniques: Downscaling whole-slide images (WSIs) to 1024 × 1024 pixels with white padding for the baseline CNN.
Splitting WSIs into non-overlapping tiles of 224 × 224 pixels for the Multiple Instance Learning (MIL) architecture.
Removing empty tiles via pixel statistics (calculating average color intensity and comparing to maximum intensity of the WSI; patches with average intensity > 0.95 × max were removed).
Normalizing nonempty tiles to 0 mean and unit variance within mini-batches of 32.
Removing the softmax function from the VGG11 feature constructor to obtain 1000-dimensional representations.
conclusions: Histopathological images can be efficiently and interpretably analyzed by state-of-the-art machine learning techniques, specifically using an attention-based multiple instance learning (MIL) classifier.
The proposed attention-ANN accurately identified basal cell carcinoma (BCC) tumor regions with high diagnostic performance (AUC: 0.993, sensitivity: 0.965, specificity: 0.910).
Machine learning algorithms rely on significantly different recognition patterns for tumor identification compared to expert pathologists (p < 10^-4).
While pathologists focused on epidermal structures, the ANN paid additional attention to connective tissue patterns.
Neural networks and machine learning algorithms have the potential to enhance diagnostic precision in digital pathology and uncover hitherto unused classification patterns.
Non-BCC samples obtained from skin in proximity to BCCs were more often classified as BCCs than other groups, although this trend was not statistically significant.

The baseline CNN using downscaled whole-slide images resulted in lower accuracy (mean accuracy 0.753) compared to the tiled MIL approach, highlighting the benefit of processing tiles for detailed feature extraction.
important_remarks: The study is a proof-of-concept using a retrospective dataset of 820 WSIs from only two Austrian hospitals, limiting generalizability and raising concerns about geographic and demographic bias.
The dataset consists of weakly labeled data (slide-level labels) rather than pixel-level annotations, which may introduce noise or ambiguity in the ground truth.

The 'normal skin' control group included samples taken from skin in proximity to other neoplasms (BCC, SCC, melanoma) or scars, which may not represent truly healthy skin and could bias the model to learn proximity-related artifacts.

Non-BCC samples obtained from skin in proximity to BCCs were more often misclassified as BCCs, suggesting the model may rely on spatial context or adjacent tumor features rather than intrinsic histological patterns of the target region.

The baseline CNN using downscaled whole-slide images (1024x1024 pixels) performed significantly worse (accuracy 0.753) than the tiled MIL approach, highlighting the critical importance of tile-based processing for gigapixel images.

The ANN relies on significantly different recognition patterns than pathologists (p < 10^-4); pathologists focused on epidermal structures and retraction artifacts, while the ANN paid additional attention to connective tissue patterns.
The study did not perform external validation on an independent dataset from a different institution or scanner type, which is a major limitation for assessing clinical robustness.
The eye-tracking study comparing ANN attention to pathologist gaze was limited to a small subset of 9 images (5 BCC, 4 non-BCC), which may not be representative of the full dataset's complexity.
The model was trained to distinguish only BCC from non-BCC, whereas real-world pathology involves distinguishing multiple tumor entities, which may explain the distinct attention patterns observed.

Legal and medico-legal responsibilities for AI-assisted diagnosis in digital pathology are not clearly regulated, posing a barrier to clinical deployment despite the high diagnostic accuracy (AUC 0.993).
